items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.

items.q: Why is the electric field between parallel charged plates considered uniform?
items.correct: Field strength is constant in magnitude and direction throughout the region (away from edges), unlike the inverse-square field of a point charge.
items.misconception: The field still decreases with the inverse square of distance — uniform just means it's smooth.
items.random_wrong: Because the plates are uniform in shape.
items.why: Students extrapolate inverse-square thinking everywhere; they don't distinguish point sources from extended geometries.

items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: Which part of the eye senses light?
items.why: It's Retina. “Lens” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which EM wave has more energy than visible light?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ultraviolet. “Radio” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Roughly, what is the speed of light in millions of metres per second?
items.why: The real figure is 300. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
